target: data/predictions.json PRED-SR-SUPPRESS — detail_reasoning and tldr rewrite

replacement_fields.detail_reasoning:
CRITICAL TIMING UPDATE: A G3 (Strong) geomagnetic storm occurred March 20-22, 2026 (NOAA SWPC confirmed, Kp reached 7). PRED-SR-SUPPRESS was registered March 23 — just 1-3 days later. Schumann resonance monitoring data is available in near-real-time, meaning the author had access to the Schumann amplitude response during the March 20-22 G3 event before registering this prediction.

An important counterargument deserves direct engagement: observing a phenomenon once and predicting it will recur is legitimate inductive science. If the author observed SR suppression during the March G3 storm and then predicted recurrence in the next G3, that follows standard scientific methodology — Kepler observed orbital paths before formulating laws that predicted future positions. We do not dispute this.

However, for PRED-SR-SUPPRESS to count as evidence for the dome model specifically, the prediction must be derived from dome physics — not from empirical induction alone. The dome's live-power dashboard (Domain 9) states the falsification criterion ("G3+ storm does NOT cause >30% amplitude drop within 6 hours") but provides no equation or mechanism from dome geometry that predicts suppression. No derivation connects the dome's constants (H₀=8,537 km, λg=8,619 km) to a suppression effect. If the prediction is empirically induced from one observation rather than model-derived, then confirming it proves the observation is repeatable — not that the dome model explains it.

The literature context also matters. Rosales-Linares et al. (2019), analyzing Schumann resonance data during geomagnetic storms at a Mexican station, found statistically significant amplitude enhancement across all five G2-G4 events studied (DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006). Enhancement during storms — driven by energetic electron precipitation modifying the Earth-ionosphere waveguide — is the more frequently reported effect, though results do vary by storm phase, station location, and measurement methodology. The dome predicts the opposite direction (suppression), which means if the March G3 showed the commonly reported enhancement, the author registered a prediction contradicting the most recent empirical evidence.

The dome's own monitoring dashboard compounds the problem. Domain 9 (Schumann Amplitude Suppression) currently reads "Unscored — no G3+ storm observed" despite NOAA SWPC confirmation of G3 levels during March 20-22. Either the dome's Kp-detection code has a bug, or the author chose not to score this event. Both undermine the narrative of fully automated, no-cherry-picking verification — the monitor built to test this prediction cannot even detect the relevant storm class.

Verdict remains pending: no G3+ events have occurred since registration that the dome's own system acknowledges. But the prospective credibility is substantially weakened by the combination of suspicious timing, absent model derivation, and a monitor that appears blind to the very events it should be tracking.
replacement_fields.tldr: Predicts G3+ storms will suppress Schumann resonance by 30% — but the dome provides no equation or mechanism that derives suppression from its model constants. A G3 storm occurred just 1-3 days before registration, raising the possibility this was observed first and registered after. Published research (Rosales-Linares et al. 2019) more commonly finds enhancement, not suppression, and the dome's own monitor reports "no G3+ storm observed" for the March event despite NOAA confirmation.

holes_addressed: ISS-923: Now explicitly acknowledges single-observation induction as legitimate science before explaining why it still fails as dome evidence (must be model-derived, not empirically induced)
ISS-925: Replaced vague ScienceDirect 2019 Mexico station citation with specific paper: Rosales-Linares et al. 2019, J. Atmos. Sol.-Terr. Phys., DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006
Softened literature claims from more commonly observed to more frequently reported with nuance about storm phase and station dependence
Retained monitor discrepancy (Domain 9 unscored) from ISS-924 update
new_evidence_added: Rosales-Linares et al. (2019) DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2018.08.006 — 5 storm events all showed SR amplitude enhancement at Mexican station
Explicit observation that dome provides no derivation connecting H0/lambda_g constants to suppression prediction

anticipated_objections.objection: Observing once and predicting recurrence IS legitimate science — you are being unfair
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly acknowledge this. The issue is not whether induction is valid, but whether inductive confirmation supports the dome MODEL versus simply confirming an observable pattern. If dome equations derive suppression, the timing is less important. If they do not (and the dome page shows no such derivation), confirmation proves repeatability, not dome theory.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Mexican station study is only one paper — the literature is not settled
anticipated_objections.response: Correct, and we now note this explicitly: results vary by storm phase, station location, and methodology. We cite Rosales-Linares as the most relevant specific paper (5 G2-G4 events, all enhancement) rather than claiming universal consensus. The point is that suppression as a prediction direction contradicts the majority-reported direction.

anticipated_objections.objection: The March storm could have shown suppression, validating the prediction informally
anticipated_objections.response: If so, the author had that data before registration, making this post-hoc. And the dome monitor itself reports no G3+ storm observed — so the dome did not score it. Either the data contradicted the prediction (and was not scored) or the monitor failed to detect the storm.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome model DOES predict suppression through cavity perturbation effects
anticipated_objections.response: If so, the derivation should be documented. Domain 9 states only a falsification criterion, not a mechanism. If the dome author publishes a derivation connecting H0/lambda_g to cavity-mode suppression during Kp perturbation, we will reassess.

kernel_of_truth: Single-observation induction IS standard scientific methodology. If the dome model independently derives SR suppression from its cavity geometry, the timing of registration is less important — the prediction would have theoretical backing regardless of when the author learned about it. We acknowledge this explicitly and explain precisely where the argument fails: no such derivation exists on the dome site.
